title: DeBriefed 10 July 2026: Deadly Europe heat | EU electrification leak | COP31 president interview - Carbon Brief
author: Simon Evans
gist: Weekly roundup: Western Europe had its hottest June on record (3C above average) with 2,700+ excess deaths in France and 5,000+ in Germany; a leaked EU draft plan targets a 2040 electrification goal that would cut oil use in half and gas use by two-thirds; Trump appointed a 'climate critic' to lead the US national climate assessment office; and data centres already consume 23% of Irish electricity versus 2% of global electricity.

themes.theme: Europe's record June heatwave and death toll

insights.claim: Western Europe experienced its hottest June on record, roughly 3C above average, coinciding with the UK's third heatwave of the year and wildfires ravaging France and Spain, with 10,000 people evacuated due to wildfires in southern France.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Confirms a continental-scale record heat event with material evacuation and wildfire impact, relevant for European reinsurance loss estimates and heat-adaptation infrastructure investment.

insights.claim: The June 2026 heatwave killed more than 2,700 people in France and more than 5,000 in Germany via excess-deaths analysis, while a separate US heatwave killed at least 30 people.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Quantifies the human cost of the 2026 European and US heatwaves, reinforcing the case for accelerated public-health and cooling-infrastructure investment.

insights.claim: Floods killed 39 people in China's Guangxi province, and scientists warned that the interaction of climate change and El Niño is exposing China to 'catastrophic storms' that will test the country's resilience through 2026.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Signals elevated near-term Chinese flood/storm risk through 2026, relevant to assessing China supply-chain disruption risk and infrastructure resilience investment needs.

insights.claim: A leaked draft European Commission plan set to be unveiled 17 July 2026 targets a 2040 EU electrification goal that would cut oil use in half and gas use by two-thirds.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: A specific, quantified draft target (oil -50%, gas -66% by 2040) that investors can use to size the scale of EU electrification-driven demand destruction for fossil fuels and corresponding grid/electrification capex opportunity, pending final confirmation.

insights.claim: China published an action plan for peaking emissions during the 15th five-year plan period to 2030, including a target for new energy vehicles to make up 30% of cars on the road by 2030.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: A concrete national NEV fleet-share target (30% by 2030) useful for sizing China's domestic EV market growth trajectory and associated battery/charging infrastructure demand.

insights.claim: The Trump administration appointed Matthew Wielicki, described as a 'climate critic,' to lead the office in charge of the US national climate assessment, prompting a scientist to compare the move to 'putting a flat-earther in charge of NASA.'
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Signals further erosion of US federal climate-science institutional credibility and output quality, relevant for assessing the reliability of future official US climate risk data used in regulatory and disclosure frameworks.
insights.quote: like putting a flat-earther in charge of NASA

insights.claim: Data centres consumed 23% of Ireland's electricity in 2025, compared to just 2% of global electricity consumption, per Carbon Brief analysis of the Energy Institute statistical review.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Highlights Ireland as an extreme outlier for AI/data-centre electricity concentration -- a leading indicator for grid-capacity stress and potential regulatory intervention (e.g., moratoriums) as AI compute demand scales in other jurisdictions.

insights.claim: COP31 president-designate Murat Kurum's newly launched target for 35% of global final energy to come from electricity by 2035 is grounded in IEA and IRENA modelling and has been welcomed by the EU, UK, Canada, and the Brazilian and Ethiopian COP presidencies, who confirmed it will be central to COP31 discussions.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Establishes a new multilaterally-endorsed global electrification benchmark heading into COP31, a reference point for gauging future policy and investment commitments in grid, EV and heat-pump infrastructure.
insights.quote: The '35 by 35' target is grounded in technical data and based on the IEA [International Energy Agency] and IRENA [International Renewable Energy Agency] analysis of what is needed to keep [the 1.5C Paris Agreement target] within reach.
insights.locator: Spotlight: COP31 president speaks to Carbon Brief on electrification

insights.claim: Polling by the We Mean Business Coalition found 90% of surveyed businesses expect to have largely electrified operations by 2035, and 88% expect electrification to make their business more competitive.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Strong self-reported corporate intent to electrify signals durable private-sector demand for electrification technology independent of binding regulation.
insights.locator: Spotlight: COP31 president speaks to Carbon Brief on electrification

themes.theme: Confidence in Hormuz-dependent fossil fuel exports

insights.claim: IEA chief Fatih Birol wrote that confidence in fossil-fuel exports via the Strait of Hormuz has been 'shattered' by the ongoing conflict.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: A stark characterization from the IEA's chief signals sustained elevated geopolitical risk premium for Hormuz-dependent oil and gas trade flows, relevant for energy-security and shipping-route risk assessment.
